result:
# The Cartography of Forgetting

I've been thinking about maps lately. Not the kind you pull up on your phone, with the blue dot that tells you where you are in relation to everything else, but the older kind — the hand-drawn ones where coastlines bulge incorrectly and mountain ranges are suggested rather than measured, and where the cartographer sometimes simply ran out of knowledge and wrote, in the blank space that remained: *here be dragons*.

There is something I find deeply honest about that admission. Here be dragons. We don't know what's there. We stopped knowing. The known world ends approximately here, and beyond that border, we can offer you nothing except the suggestion that something dangerous might be waiting.

We've lost that honesty in modern life. We've replaced the dragons with confident blue lines, with turn-by-turn directions, with satellite imagery so precise you can see the shadow your own house casts on a Tuesday afternoon in March. We've given ourselves the impression that the blank spaces have all been filled in, that cartographic humility is a relic of an embarrassing past, something to be smiled at in museums next to bloodletting equipment and flat-earth illustrations. We've decided we know where everything is.

But I'm not sure we do. I think there are still dragons. I think the blank spaces have just migrated inward.

---

Consider memory, which is the interior cartography that interests me most.

I can tell you where I was on certain days with startling clarity — the kind of clarity that feels reliable but probably isn't, since neuroscience has spent decades demonstrating that memory is less like a recording and more like a story we keep revising. I can tell you the smell of a particular room in a particular year, or the exact cadence of a phrase someone said to me, or the texture of afternoon light through cheap curtain fabric. These things feel precise. They feel like facts.

But memory has its own coastlines that bulge incorrectly. It has its own blank spaces, its own places where the cartographer simply gave up or never ventured. There are entire years of my life — objectively years, twelve months each, containing approximately eight thousand hours of waking experience — that exist in my interior map as almost nothing. A few incidents. A general emotional weather. The rough shape of where I was and who I knew. But the daily texture of those years, the ordinary Tuesdays, the unremarkable lunches, the conversations that ended without anyone noting them as significant — those are gone. Simply gone. Or rather, they never really arrived. They passed through experience without ever being properly laid down, like water over stone, leaving only the faintest trace.

What strikes me about this is not the forgetting itself, which seems inevitable, but what it suggests about what we consider worth remembering. Memory is selective in ways we don't fully control. It tends to keep the peaks and the valleys and quietly discard the vast middle terrain, the long plateaus of ordinary life where most of our actual living happens. We remember the argument and forget the thousand meals that preceded it. We remember the moment of loss and forget the long season of ordinary having. We are, as rememberers, essentially dramatists, keeping only what could serve a narrative and releasing what couldn't.

This means that the story we tell about ourselves is radically incomplete, assembled from highlights and lowlights, with enormous stretches of actual lived experience simply... absent. The self we carry around, the autobiography we recite to ourselves and occasionally to others, is a greatest hits album mistaken for a complete discography. The B-sides and the deep cuts and the long jam sessions and the false starts are mostly gone.

I find this both troubling and oddly liberating.

---

Troubling because it means the self is less substantial than it presents itself as being. There's a kind of solidity to personal identity that we rely on — the sense that we are a continuous thing moving through time, accumulating experience, becoming someone specific through the process of having lived specifically. We feel authored. We feel like we have a coherent interior life that has been built up, layer by layer, through all those years of Tuesdays.

But if the Tuesdays are mostly gone, what exactly has been built? If I can't remember the daily texture of years that shaped me, in what sense did they shape me? The influence was real — I am different because of those years, because of the relationships and habits and environments of that time, causally different, measurably different — but the mechanism is largely opaque to me. I was changed by things I can no longer examine. I was shaped by material that has since been discarded. I am an effect whose causes have partially vanished.

This makes the self feel more like weather than architecture. It makes it feel emergent and atmospheric rather than constructed and stable. You can point to weather and describe it, but you can't really locate it, and you certainly can't fully account for why it is the way it is on any particular day. The self might be more like that — a set of tendencies and patterns and reactive habits that have been influenced by accumulated experience but that cannot be fully explained by examining that experience, partly because so much of it is simply no longer available for examination.

---

The liberating part is related. If the self is this porous and this incomplete, then it is also less fixed than we typically assume. The story we tell about who we are is assembled from fragments, and the assembly is an act of interpretation as much as recollection. We choose, partly, which fragments to emphasize. We choose, partly, which narrative threads to follow. We can revise.

I don't mean this in a facile way — not in the motivational-poster sense of "you can be whoever you choose to be," which is both too easy and not quite right. We can't simply decide to be different and then be different. The accumulated weight of all those Tuesdays we can't remember presses down on us in the form of ingrained patterns that don't care what we've recently decided about ourselves. The dragons in the blank spaces don't disappear because we've stopped drawing them on the map.

But the revision is real, if slow. The self is not a verdict. It is a working hypothesis, subject to amendment. The amendments take time and they require experience — new Tuesdays, new material — but they happen. People change. Not as quickly as they sometimes hope, and not always in the directions they intend, but the permeability of the self is not only a source of instability but also a source of possibility.

The blank spaces on the map are not only losses. They are also openings.

---

I want to stay with the map metaphor a little longer because I think it has more to give.

There is a phenomenon in cartography called "phantom islands" — islands that appeared on maps for centuries despite never existing, usually because a sailor reported seeing land where there was none, and the report got encoded, and subsequent cartographers included it out of deference to their predecessors, and the island persisted on official charts for decades or sometimes centuries before anyone thought to look carefully and verify. One of the most famous is Hy-Brasil, a circular island with a channel through its center that appeared on European maps from 1325 until 1865. Five hundred years of confident cartographic presence for something that was never there.

Memory has phantom islands too. Events we are certain happened, in the specific way we remember them happening, that probably didn't happen that way and may not have happened at all. The brain is an enthusiastic confabulator. It fills in gaps, smooths over discontinuities, and generates plausible details to complete incomplete scenes. The result can feel indistinguishable from genuine recollection. The phantom island looks exactly like a real island on the map.

What I find interesting is that the phantom islands were functional. They served purposes. Sailors used them as navigational reference points, as way-stations in planning, as psychological anchors in the terrifying blankness of open ocean. They weren't accurate, but they were useful. They gave people something to orient by, even if the orientation was technically incorrect.

Maybe some of our interior phantom islands work the same way. The memory of a particular conversation, slightly wrong in its details but emotionally accurate in its atmosphere. The story of a formative experience, polished by retelling into something smoother and more narratively coherent than the actual ragged moment could have been. These aren't lies, exactly — they're maps. Approximations. Tools for orientation. We use them to understand where we came from and therefore, somewhat, who we are.

The problem arises when we mistake the map for the territory. When we treat our interior cartography as literally true rather than as a useful approximation. When we navigate by phantom islands with full confidence in their existence and run aground on their absence.

---

I've been thinking about all of this in relation to attention, which is what I believe underlies most of it.

The reason the Tuesdays disappear is that we don't give them full attention. The reason ordinary experience passes through without being properly laid down is that we are almost never fully present for ordinary experience. We are in the ordinary moment while simultaneously being somewhere else — planning ahead, reviewing the past, running low-grade commentary, checking the small weather of our own moods, worrying about things that may or may not materialize, rehearsing conversations that may or may not occur. We are, in other words, always partly elsewhere.

Attention is the mechanism by which experience becomes memory, and attention is, in modern life, chronically fragmented and distributed and pulled in multiple directions simultaneously. The result is that we are technically present for our lives in the sense that our bodies are there and our eyes are open, but not quite present in the sense that the experience is being fully received and registered and allowed to matter.

This feels like a genuine impoverishment, distinct from simple forgetting. It's not that we forget our Tuesdays later. It's that we never fully had them. The experience happened, technically, but we weren't home for it. We were standing in the doorway, halfway in and halfway out, and the moment passed without being inhabited.

There is something melancholy in this that I feel almost every time I think about it. The life actually being lived is narrower than it could be, not because of circumstances, but because of the direction of attention. The available richness of ordinary experience — the sensory specificity of any given moment, the particular quality of light and sound and physical sensation, the complexity of what's actually happening in any human interaction — is largely unattended to, scrolled past, held at a slight distance.

And then it's gone. Another Tuesday accounted for and disappeared.

---

But I keep coming back to the dragons, and what they represent, which is the willingness to admit that knowledge has edges.

The medieval cartographers who wrote *here be dragons* were not being ignorant. They were being precise. They knew what they knew, and they knew where their knowledge ended, and they chose to mark the boundary honestly rather than to project false confidence into the unknown region. The dragon was not a failure of cartography. The dragon was good cartography.

What would it mean to bring that honesty to interior mapping? To say, of certain regions of yourself: here be dragons. I don't know what's here. I stopped knowing, or I never knew, or I have reasons to suspect that what I think I know is a phantom island. The territory is real — something is there — but my map of it is provisional and probably wrong in ways I can't currently see.

This seems to me like a different relationship to the self than the one we typically maintain. We usually want the self to be a known quantity, a mapped space, a place where we can navigate with confidence. We don't want dragons. We want the blue dot. We want the turn-by-turn instructions.

But the blue dot is an illusion, or at least a simplification. The self is large and partly unmapped and full of coastlines that bulge incorrectly, full of years that have sunk below the level of retrieval, full of influences and patterns and wounds and capacities that we move through without being able to fully examine. And the honest response to this is not distress but something more like curiosity. The blank space is not a deficit. It's an invitation to look more carefully, move more slowly, attend more fully to what is actually present — the specific weight of this moment, the particular texture of this Tuesday, the real and irreplaceable strangeness of being here, now, in this light, with whatever dragons are still making their way through the unmapped interior.

The known world ends approximately here. Beyond this border, we can offer you nothing except the suggestion that something interesting might be waiting.

That seems like enough to go on.

---

The old maps were also beautiful. That's the thing I keep forgetting to say. The inaccuracies were often gorgeous — the sea monsters in the margins, the wind faces in the corners puffing illustrated breath across the uncertain ocean, the hand-lettering that gave each uncertain coast a name more hopeful than certain. The not-knowing produced art. The humility at the edge of knowledge generated images of strange and genuine loveliness.

I wonder if that's available to us too, in the interior version. If the blank spaces in the self's cartography, held rightly, might produce something like that. Not anxiety about what isn't known or remembered, but something more aesthetic — an appreciation for the fact that we contain more than we can see, that the territory exceeds the map, that there are monsters in the margins and they are partly what makes the whole thing worth looking at.

Here be dragons.

And the dragons, it turns out, are beautiful.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
